A German-accented voice, likely portraying Whitehead, then declares, "I have no doubt, flying machines will be as common as automobiles in a few years time," continuing, "And before you and I are old men, there will be more traveling through the air than there are on land or water." Archival footage of a biplane in flight and a modern commercial airliner soaring above clouds symbolizes aviation’s evolution. The man in the bowler hat is then seen in a rustic workshop, speaking with a serious expression, as the German-accented voice continues his predictions. A top-down view of a large, multi-winged flying machine is shown. The British-accented narrator returns, stating, "But back in 1902, Whitehead needed money to make his plane commercially viable. Unlike the Wright brothers, he was unlucky in business." The man in the bowler hat walks outside a weathered wooden building, knocking on a heavy door with urgency, as the narrator adds, "An investor called in a loan." Dramatic shouting, including "Mark off!" and "This is my shop!", along with a loud bang, accompanies the man peering through the door’s crack with a tense expression, before the narrator concludes, "Locked out."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a somber orchestral score as a man in a dark coat and bowler hat, visually representing Gustav Whitehead, is seen first behind vertical bars, then walking away from an early biplane in a sunlit field, embodying his story of being lost to history. A male narrator recounts how 'Whitehead never fully recovered' and how 'the German aviator had disappeared into the fog of history' by the time Stella Randolf researched his 'lost flights.' The narrator explains that Whitehead 'spent the later years of his life constructing aviation engines' and 'died in relative obscurity' in 1927, buried in an unmarked pauper's grave, accompanied by a mournful bell sound. The scene shifts to a grainy black-and-white photograph of a man holding a tower model, followed by archival footage of an early biplane on a sandy dune, lifting off and flying low, with mechanical whirring and engine sounds. The narrator then transitions, stating, 'And so we are left with the story of the Wright Brothers,' as the plane continues to fly, its wooden structure visible. A modern, colorized reenactment shows the aircraft taxiing across a grassy field with a pilot in a white shirt at the controls, accompanied by loud engine roars and propeller sounds. The narrator concludes by noting that since the Wright Brothers 'first flew in front of a movie camera, we have believed their version of history... that the Wright Brothers flew first at Kitty Hawk in 1903.' The segment ends with a wide shot of the Wright Brothers National Memorial, inscribed with 'ORVILLE WRIGHT,' under an overcast sky, solidifying the commemoration of their legacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a low-angle shot of the Wright Brothers National Memorial, a tall stone monument inscribed with 'ORVILLE WRIGHT,' set against an overcast sky, as a male narrator states with a serious, authoritative tone that "the standing of the rights is as formidable as ever, handed down through generations." A female voice then recites, "Achieved by dauntless resolution and unconquerable faith," as the scene transitions to visitors, including children, walking toward the monument. The male narrator continues, mentioning the Wrights were "protected by a contract signed by the Smithsonian Secretary on behalf of the United States of America." A museum guide in a gray shirt and dark pants stands beside the Wright Flyer, a biplane with a wooden frame and fabric wings, explaining its features to visitors in a well-lit exhibit. The camera then shifts to the grand redstone Smithsonian Institution building, followed by a close-up of a formal 'AGREEMENT' document detailing the transfer of the Wright Flyer to the USA, signed by Harold S. Miller and the Smithsonian Secretary on November 23, 1948. Dr. Tom Crouch, an elderly man with a bald head, white mustache, and glasses, wearing a dark suit, then speaks directly, his clear and measured voice discussing "creditable evidence" that someone flew before the Wrights, stating he would "admit it." A distinct whirring/engine sound begins as archival footage shows a large, white-winged flying machine being transported through a wooded area. A second male narrator, with an academic tone, explains that "seven separate flights" have been documented in "contemporary newspapers and magazines" predating the Wrights' first claimed flight, as a black-and-white newspaper clipping from the 'Pittsburgh Leader' about Gustave Whitehead’s experiments is shown. A close-up of a handwritten affidavit by Michael Werer from September 1934 is displayed, followed by a reenactment of a man in a pinstripe suit signing a document. The second narrator continues, mentioning "17 witness or testimonial sources," emphasizing "This is way beyond a shadow of a doubt" and that "The current history of aviation needs to be re-evaluated," concluding with "Who flew..." as a modern reenactment of an early, multi-winged flying machine with multiple propellers is shown in flight over a grassy field, accompanied by the persistent whirring/engine sound.

The driving, suspenseful orchestral music continues as credits roll over a serene sky filled with clouds, listing production roles like 'Written & Directed by TILMAN REMME' and 'Editor NICHOLAS DUNLOP,' along with logos for ScreenWest, LotteryWest, Artemis International, ABC, 2DF, and Screen Australia.
